text.title: Status Report Templates by Audience Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that guarantee each audience gets a report shaped for the way they read. Each rule is verifiable from the PM's audience map and report archive — no subjective judgement required.

rule.statement: Every stakeholder receiving the weekly status MUST be mapped to ONE of four templates: CEO (3-number + 1-risk, <100 words), PMO (RAG table + plan delta, ≤300 words), Technical Sponsor (scope + blockers + decisions needed, ≤400 words), Internal Leadership (narrative + ask + confidence, ≤500 words). A stakeholder receiving "all of the above" is forbidden — pick one.

rationale.source: Stakeholder communication literature; Mike Cohn status reporting patterns

rule.rationale: Different roles read for different signals. A CEO will not scroll past the first three lines; a technical sponsor needs the blocker detail the CEO would skip. Merging the four into one report optimises for nobody and is the modal failure mode.

rule.statement: All four templates MUST be generated from ONE shared spreadsheet / Notion DB (milestone, RAG, owner, due date, blocker note, budget). The PM MUST NOT hand-edit the four reports independently. Generation can be manual copy-paste or scripted, but the source is single.
rule.rationale: Maintaining four parallel narratives drifts within one cycle: the CEO report says "on track" while the PMO report says AMBER. A single source enforces consistency by construction and cuts production time roughly fourfold.

rule.statement: The CEO template MUST fit in 5 lines or fewer in a plain-text email body: 1 line state ("On plan", "Slipping", "At risk"), 3 numbers (e.g. progress %, budget %, weeks to next milestone), 1 risk or ask. Attachments allowed but optional. If 5 lines cannot tell the truth, escalate via `escalation-decision-template` instead.
rule.rationale: CEOs read inbox previews. The 5-line cap forces the PM to write what fits in the preview pane. The discipline of compressing weekly state to 5 lines is itself a quality check: if the PM can't do it, they don't have a clear picture either.

rule.statement: The PMO template MUST lead with a RAG (Red / Amber / Green) status table covering all active workstreams BEFORE any narrative. Prose follows the table to explain Amber / Red rows. Green rows get no prose; absence of explanation is the signal of "no news, on track".

rationale.source: PMO communication conventions; Prince2 status reporting

rule.rationale: PMOs scan RAG dashboards. Narrative buried before the table forces them to extract the dashboard themselves, which they will not do — they will ask the PM in the next stand-up. Leading with RAG saves both parties a meeting.

rule.statement: Technical scope, dependency, and architectural decisions MUST appear in the Technical Sponsor template ONLY. The Internal Leadership template MUST translate those into business impact ("Auth migration completed; users now SSO-able with no support tickets") and MUST NOT contain raw technical language or component names.

rationale.source: Translation discipline; common cross-functional failure mode

rule.rationale: Putting technical detail in the leadership report tells the VP "this PM cannot translate" — eroding the trust the report was meant to build. The split keeps each audience inside its competence band and signals the PM understands theirs.

example.title: CEO template — Week 7 of 12, mid-flight project
example.code:
Subject: Acme migration — Week 7 — On plan
State: On plan. Numbers: 58% complete · 51% of budget · 5 weeks to UAT. Risk: Vendor SSO library on hold pending their security review (no schedule impact yet; will impact week 9 if not resolved).
example.note: r3 satisfied — 5 lines, 3 numbers, 1 risk. CEO knows in 8 seconds whether to keep scrolling.

example.title: One-size-fits-all report sent to everyone
example.code:
Subject: Weekly status
This week we made good progress on multiple workstreams. The team had a great stand-up on Tuesday where we discussed the Postgres migration in depth, and we identified that the read-replica lag will need to be addressed in week 9. The SSO library issue from last week is still pending vendor review... [continues for 1,400 words covering everything for everyone]
example.note: r1, r3, r4, r5 violations. CEO will not read past line 2. PMO won't find the RAG. Sponsor's blocker detail is buried. VP gets raw technical language. Nobody opens this report by week 4.
